lede: Good morning! Google just dropped Gemini 3.1 Flash Live with more natural audio AI, and they're rolling out Search Live globally. Meanwhile, someone built a $500 GPU setup that beats Claude Sonnet on coding benchmarks, and a team used AI to rewrite JSONata in a day and claims $500k/year in savings. Plus a clever AI agent running on a $7/month VPS using IRC.

quick_links.note: Essay on using oracles to validate LLM-generated code
quick_links.title: Taming LLMs: Using executable checks to prevent bad code

quick_links.note: Interactive essay on how model quantization works
quick_links.title: Quantization explained from the ground up

quick_links.note: Latest episode of Google's Dialogues series
quick_links.title: LL COOL J. and James Manyika discuss AI and creativity

items.body: Google's new Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is now available across Google products, promising more natural and reliable audio interactions. This is Google's push to make voice-based AI feel less robotic and more conversational. If you've been frustrated with clunky voice AI, this might be worth testing.
items.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live makes audio AI more natural

items.body: Google's Search Live feature is now available in all languages and locations where AI Mode is available. This is the conversational search interface that lets you ask follow-up questions naturally. Big accessibility win for non-English speakers.
items.title: Search Live expanding globally

items.body: Google Translate's live translation feature for headphones is officially on iOS now, and expanding to more countries on both platforms. Pop in your earbuds and get real-time translation of conversations around you. Practical for travel or multilingual meetings.
items.title: Live translation in your headphones comes to iOS

items.body: Someone claims their $500 GPU setup beats Claude Sonnet on coding tasks. The GitHub repo is called ATLAS, though details are light. If true, this is a big deal for anyone who wants to run powerful coding assistance locally without subscriptions. Lots of skepticism and questions on HN about methodology, so take with a grain of salt until more testing.
items.title: $500 GPU outperforms Claude Sonnet on coding benchmarks

items.body: This is legitimately clever: someone built an AI agent that lives on a cheap VPS and uses IRC (yes, the chat protocol from the '90s) as its communication layer. It's a great example of using old, reliable protocols for new AI workflows. The writeup walks through the whole architecture and why IRC actually makes sense here.
items.title: AI agent on a $7/month VPS using IRC as transport

items.body: A new TypeScript library for extracting structured data from websites using LLMs. If you've been scraping sites and wrestling with brittle selectors, this takes a different approach by letting the model figure out what to extract. Worth a look if you're building anything that needs to pull data from varied web sources.
items.title: Robust LLM extractor for websites in TypeScript

items.body: A team at Reco used AI to port JSONata (a JSON query language) from JavaScript to Go in a single day, claiming it saves them $500k annually in infrastructure costs. This is another case study in "vibe porting" — using AI to rewrite code in a different language without fully understanding every detail. Simon Willison has good context on what makes this interesting beyond the clickbait framing.
items.title: Team rewrote JSONata with AI in a day, claims $500k/year savings

items.body: Callum McMahon shares his minute-by-minute response to discovering malware in the LiteLLM Python package, including the Claude transcripts he used to confirm the vulnerability and decide what to do. It's a fascinating look at using AI as a thinking partner during a security incident. Claude even suggested the right PyPI security contact.
items.title: Responding to a supply chain attack with Claude's help

slack_text:
🌞 Good morning, NOLA! Friday, March 27, 2026. Today's vibe: Google's shipping product updates while builders are getting creative with cheap infrastructure.

---

🚀 *Big Releases*

• <https://blog.google/innovation-and-ai/models-and-research/gemini-models/gemini-3-1-flash-live/|Gemini 3.1 Flash Live> just dropped with more natural audio AI. Google's pushing to make voice interactions feel less robotic — now available across their products.

• <https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/search/search-live-global-expansion/|Search Live is going global>, expanding to all languages where AI Mode works. Conversational search for everyone.

• <https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/translate/live-translate-with-headphones/|Live translation in your headphones> is officially on iOS now. Pop in your earbuds and get real-time translation.

---

🛠️ *Things People Built*

• Someone claims their <https://github.com/itigges22/ATLAS|$500 GPU setup beats Claude Sonnet on coding>. The HN thread is full of healthy skepticism about methodology, but if even half-true, this is interesting for local development.

• This is clever: <https://georgelarson.me/writing/2026-03-23-nullclaw-doorman/|an AI agent running on a $7/month VPS using IRC> as its transport layer. Sometimes the old protocols are the best protocols.

• New <https://github.com/lightfeed/extractor|TypeScript library for extracting data from websites> using LLMs. If you're tired of brittle CSS selectors, this takes a different approach.

---

💼 *How Teams Are Using AI*

• Reco claims they <https://www.reco.ai/blog/we-rewrote-jsonata-with-ai|used AI to rewrite JSONata in a day> and saved $500k/year in infrastructure costs. Hyperbolic framing aside, it's another solid case study in "vibe porting" code between languages.

• Great writeup: <https://simonwillison.net/2026/Mar/26/response-to-the-litellm-malware-attack/|responding to a supply chain attack with Claude's help>. Minute-by-minute transcript of using AI as a thinking partner during a security incident.
